Our Home

Rooted in London

MIRA.CO was founded in the heart of East London — where creativity, culture, and craft collide. Our studio sits between Shoreditch's independent boutiques and the City's quiet lanes, drawing inspiration from both.

We believe London dressing is effortless but intentional — quality over quantity, texture over trend. Our pieces are designed to work with London's unpredictable weather and its equally unpredictable social calendar.

Annual Collections
Who We Are

More than a label

"Clothing that makes you feel considered — like the city itself on a rare, golden afternoon."

MIRA.CO is a London-based contemporary clothing brand founded on the belief that what you wear should feel as considered as how you live. We work with a small collective of skilled craftspeople — from the wool mills of Yorkshire to the knitwear ateliers of Kathmandu — to produce pieces of genuine quality.

Our aesthetic is defined by restraint: tonal palettes, clean silhouettes, and fabrics that feel better the more you wear them. No excess, no noise — just clothes that belong in your life for years.

Explore the Collection

What is Full Stack Development?

Full stack development refers to the practice of building both the front-end (what users see and interact with) and the back-end (the server, database, and application logic) of a web application. A full stack developer works across the entire technology stack.

The front-end is built with HTML (structure), CSS (styling), and JavaScript (interactivity) — the technologies that run inside the user's browser. The back-end involves a server (e.g. Node.js, Python/Django), a database (e.g. MongoDB, PostgreSQL), and an API layer that connects both sides.

For MIRA.CO, the front-end is this complete HTML/CSS/JS website. In a real deployment, the back-end would handle user accounts, product inventory stored in a database, order processing, and secure payment APIs — all invisible to the shopper but essential to the experience.

The Web Stack — MIRA.CO
🖥 Front-End: HTML · CSS · JavaScript Browser
⚙️ Back-End: Node.js / Express API Server
🗄 Database: MongoDB (Products, Orders) Data Layer

✦ Live Demonstration — Full Stack Concept

This interactive panel simulates how the front-end and back-end communicate. Click "Fetch Products" to simulate an API call from the browser (front-end) to a server (back-end) and display the returned data.

// Front-end JavaScript
fetch('/api/products')
  .then(res => res.json())
  .then(data => renderProducts(data));

// Waiting for user action...
Server Response (JSON):

HTML — Structure of the Web

HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the foundational language of every web page. It provides the semantic structure — the skeleton — that browsers parse and render. HTML uses elements (tags) to define what content is: a heading, a paragraph, an image, a navigation bar, a form.

HTML5 introduced semantic elements — tags whose names carry meaning beyond just visual layout. For example, <nav> tells browsers and screen readers "this is navigation", while <article> marks self-contained content. This improves accessibility and SEO.

In MIRA.CO, semantic HTML is used throughout — <nav> for the top bar, <section> for each content block, <footer> for the bottom, and <figure> with alt attributes on every product image.

Key HTML Concepts Used in MIRA.CO
✓ Semantic elements
✓ Forms & inputs
✓ Image alt attributes
✓ Anchor links
✓ Lists (nav menus)
✓ Meta viewport tag
✓ Google Fonts link
✓ Data attributes (JS)
✦ Live Demo — HTML Semantic Card 
<!-- Semantic HTML product card -->
<article class="product-card">

  <figure>
    <img 
      src="sweater.jpg"
      alt="Dark Olive Merino Sweater"
    />
  </figure>

  <header>
    <h3>Dark Olive Sweater</h3>
    <p class="category">Knitwear</p>
  </header>

  <footer>
    <strong>£115</strong>
    <button 
      type="button"
      aria-label="Add to cart"
    >Add to Cart</button>
  </footer>

</article>

↓ Rendered Output:

Knitwear

Dark Olive Sweater

£115

CSS — Styling the Web

CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls the visual presentation of HTML elements — colours, typography, layout, spacing, and animations. The word "cascading" refers to how styles are applied in order of priority (specificity), allowing general rules to be overridden by more specific ones.

Modern CSS features used in MIRA.CO include CSS Custom Properties (variables like --brown-deep) for consistent theming, CSS Grid for the product layout, Flexbox for navigation alignment, @keyframes for the ticker animation, and transitions for hover effects on every product card.

The entire MIRA.CO colour palette — beige, white, and warm browns — is defined as CSS variables at the :root level, meaning the whole site's look can be updated by changing a single value.

CSS Features in MIRA.CO
CSS Custom Properties (:root) Theming
CSS Grid — product cards, hero Layout
Flexbox — nav, buttons, footer Layout
@keyframes — ticker, fade animations Animation
Pseudo-elements ::before, ::after Decoration
✦ Live Demo — CSS Hover & Animation 
/* CSS Custom Properties */
:root {
  --brown-deep: #3a2410;
  --beige: #f4f0ea;
  --transition: 0.3s ease;
}

/* Hover transition on card */
.card:hover img {
  transform: scale(1.07);
  transition: transform 0.7s cubic-bezier(0.25,0.46,0.45,0.94);
}

/* Keyframe animation */
@keyframes ticker {
  from { transform: translateX(0); }
  to   { transform: translateX(-50%); }
}

/* CSS Grid layout */
.products-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr);
  gap: 1.5rem;
}

✦ Interact — Change Theme Colour:

MIRA.CO

CSS variables update the entire theme instantly — click a colour above to see it change.

Bootstrap Framework — Rapid Responsive Design

Bootstrap is an open-source front-end CSS framework — a pre-written collection of CSS classes and JavaScript components that allows developers to build responsive, mobile-first websites rapidly, without writing all the CSS from scratch.

Bootstrap's core feature is its 12-column grid system. By applying classes like col-md-6 or col-lg-3, developers can define how many columns an element should occupy at different screen sizes — making layouts automatically responsive.

Bootstrap also ships ready-made components: navbars, modals, cards, badges, accordions, and carousels. In the MIRA.CO context below, the product grid and badge components are rebuilt using Bootstrap classes to demonstrate how the same layout can be achieved via the framework.

Bootstrap

Pre-written classes, fast to build, consistent, large community. Less unique visual output.

Custom CSS

Full creative control, unique aesthetics, smaller file size. Takes longer to build.

✦ Live Demo — Bootstrap Grid & Components 
<!-- Bootstrap CDN link in <head> -->
<link rel="stylesheet"
  href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5/dist/css/bootstrap.min.css">

<!-- Bootstrap 12-column grid -->
<div class="container">
  <div class="row">
    <!-- 4 cards, each takes 3/12 cols -->
    <div class="col-12 col-sm-6 col-lg-3">
      <div class="card shadow-sm">
        <img class="card-img-top" src="sweater.jpg">
        <div class="card-body">
          <h5 class="card-title">Olive Sweater</h5>
          <span class="badge bg-dark">New</span>
          <p class="fw-bold mt-2">£115</p>
          <button class="btn btn-dark btn-sm w-100">
            Add to Cart
          </button>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

JavaScript — Making the Web Interactive

JavaScript (JS) is the programming language of the web. While HTML provides structure and CSS provides style, JavaScript provides behaviour — it makes pages dynamic and interactive by responding to user actions in real time without requiring a page reload.

JavaScript runs inside the browser and can manipulate the DOM (Document Object Model) — the live representation of the HTML structure — to add, remove, or change elements and styles dynamically. It can also make API calls (fetch) to retrieve data from servers asynchronously.

In MIRA.CO, JavaScript powers: the shopping cart (add/remove items, update count), the scroll animations (IntersectionObserver), the wishlist toggle, the custom cursor tracking, and the notification toasts that appear when actions are taken.

JS Features in MIRA.CO
→
DOM Manipulation
Updating cart count, showing/hiding drawer
→
Event Listeners
onClick, onMouseMove, scroll events
→
IntersectionObserver API
Trigger fade-in animations on scroll
→
Arrays & Functions
cartItems array, addToCart(), showNotif()
✦ Live Demo — JavaScript Shopping Cart Logic 
// Cart state — stored in an array
let cartItems = [];
let cartTotal = 0;

// Add item to cart
function addToCart(name, price) {
  cartItems.push({ name, price });
  cartTotal += price;

  // Update the DOM
  document.getElementById("cartCount")
    .textContent = cartItems.length;

  // Show notification
  showNotif("Added to cart ✓");
}

// IntersectionObserver for scroll animations
const observer = new IntersectionObserver(
  (entries) => {
    entries.forEach(e => {
      if (e.isIntersecting)
        e.target.classList.add("visible");
    });
  },
  { threshold: 0.1 }
);
